metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's file/line reference for `_verdict_manifest`: it lives in `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py` at line 161, not `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py:282`.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `_verdict_manifest` is located at `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py:282`, but the function is actually defined in `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py` at line 161. Line 282 of `transition.py` is inside the body of a `close_ticket()` call and has nothing to do with `_verdict_manifest`.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py:282` with `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:161` in the What section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the `_verdict_manifest` module reference in the plan from `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py:282` to `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:161`, where the function actually lives.
findings.finding: The plan attributes `_verdict_manifest` to `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py:282`, but that function does not exist in `transition.py` at all — it is defined at `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:161`. Line 282 of `transition.py` is a comment, not a function definition. An implementor following the plan will look in the wrong file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the file reference for `_verdict_manifest` from `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py:282` to `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:161` (the function is defined in the `transition_close` module-size seam).
findings.finding: The plan cites `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py:282` as the location of `_verdict_manifest`, but that function is defined in `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:161`; line 282 of `transition.py` is a comment block about delegating to `close_ticket`.
findings.suggested_fix: Update the plan's What section to read: `_verdict_manifest — src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:161 (completion-verifier)`.
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale in the plan explaining why `rebar-version:` is placed as a signed manifest line (entering HMAC bytes) rather than an unsigned record-level field like `head_sha` — or explicitly acknowledge the trade-off (stronger audit guarantee vs. re-sign idempotency) so implementers and downstream story authors can make consistent decisions.
findings.finding: The plan places `rebar-version:` as a manifest line, which means it enters `_canonical_payload` and is covered by the HMAC signature — yet `signing.py` has an explicit architectural comment ('The signed payload deliberately does NOT include volatile git state') that places `head_sha` OUTSIDE the signed bytes as an unsigned sidecar field. The plan provides no positive rationale for why the gate-code version stamp should be SIGNED (unlike `head_sha`) rather than stored as an unsigned record-level field. Without this rationale, an implementer may not appreciate that re-signing the same ticket content under a different rebar version produces a different signature, a meaningful side-effect that deserves explicit acknowledgement.
findings.location: What section — resolver spec + 'Append one `rebar-version:` line to BOTH manifests'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a single named prefix constant (e.g. `_REBAR_VERSION_PREFIX = "rebar-version:"`) in one canonical location (attest.py or signing.py) and explicitly specify that both `build_manifest` and `_verdict_manifest` import and use that constant — so the parser and both emitters are guaranteed to agree.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new `rebar-version:` manifest line that must be emitted by two separate functions in two separate modules (build_manifest in attest.py and _verdict_manifest in transition_close.py) and parsed by manifest_rebar_version (attest.py), but never names or locates the prefix constant for this line. The analogous constant for regver is `_REGVER_PREFIX = "regver:"` defined at the top of attest.py — but transition_close.py does NOT import that constant (it uses inline string literals like `"material:"`, `"model:"`). Without a named constant anchored in one module that all three sites import, implementors must independently choose the string and its import path, risking a silent mismatch between the emitter in transition_close.py and the parser in attest.py.
findings.location: What section / build_manifest (attest.py) + _verdict_manifest (transition_close.py:161) + manifest_rebar_version (attest.py, to be added)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `_REBAR_VERSION_PREFIX = "rebar-version:"` to the plan's 'What' section for attest.py, and add an explicit import of it in the _verdict_manifest specification in transition_close.py (matching the existing pattern where attest.py owns `_REGVER_PREFIX`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the new gate-code SHA resolver in `signing.py` should use `run_git()` from `_store/gitutil.py` (consistent with that module's consolidation mandate) or stay with the raw `subprocess.run` idiom that `head_sha()` already uses in the same file — and document the rationale in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan instructs the new gate-code SHA resolver to use a raw `subprocess.run(...)` call (reusing the `head_sha()` idiom), but `src/rebar/_store/gitutil.py` already provides `run_git()` — a consolidated wrapper whose stated purpose is to replace 'the dozen hand-rolled `_git()` wrappers that had drifted into a different signature/return/error contract each.' The new resolver would add a second raw git subprocess to `signing.py` (two calls: `rev-parse --short` and `status --porcelain`) in contradiction to `gitutil`'s mandate, even though the plan correctly forbids inventing a new wrapper.
findings.location: What / 'Add a gate-code SHA/version resolver in `src/rebar/signing.py`' + `src/rebar/_store/gitutil.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the `rebar-version:` stamp format, resolution-precedence rules (live-git-first, omit-last), and the cross-manifest contract in an ADR or CLAUDE.md section so stories 2 and 3 can build on a stable spec.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision — gate-code version stamping into attestation manifests for audit provenance — but specifies no update to an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design document to capture this decision and its format contract.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short ADR (or a `## Design notes` block in CLAUDE.md) recording: (1) the stamp format `"<version> (<short-sha>[-dirty])"`; (2) live-git-first / version-only fallback precedence; (3) that `compute_validity` must never read this field; (4) that `manifest_rebar_version()` is the sole parser — so stories 2/3 have a stable contract to extend.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all existing consumers of `build_manifest` and `_verdict_manifest` text (beyond `compute_validity`) and confirm each is tolerant of the appended `rebar-version:` line, or explicitly note that no other consumers exist.
findings.finding: The plan mutates the output format of `build_manifest` and `_verdict_manifest` but only explicitly clears `compute_validity` as a tolerant consumer; other callers that read or display these manifests (e.g., `verify_signature`, any `show`-related code) are not enumerated or confirmed tolerant of the new appended line.
findings.location: ## What — 'Append one rebar-version: line to BOTH manifests' + AC #4
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence in ## Scope or ## What: 'Audit of existing manifest consumers: only `compute_validity` reads the manifest body; `verify_signature` delegates to `compute_validity` and does not independently parse lines — no other callers require changes.' If other consumers exist, list them and assert tolerance.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that 'live-checkout resolution incl. `-dirty`' in AC5 is an integration-level test that actually invokes git (no subprocess mock), or add a separate integration test that runs the resolver in a real git checkout to prove the end-to-end path.
findings.finding: The plan groups 'live-checkout resolution incl. -dirty' under 'Unit tests', but it is unclear whether this test actually invokes the real git subprocess or mocks it. If the test mocks subprocess calls, it does not exercise the production code path (the real git invocation), meaning AC2 could pass while the live resolver silently fails in a real checkout.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5: 'Unit tests: … live-checkout resolution incl. `-dirty`'
findings.suggested_fix: Rename the AC5 bullet to 'integration test: live-checkout resolution incl. `-dirty` (real git subprocess, no mock)' and add a note that this test runs in the existing git repo context of the CI checkout, confirming the resolver returns a non-None SHA and correctly appends `-dirty` when a file is staged.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan: state whether old code gracefully ignores unknown manifest lines (and how), or specify an expand-contract sequencing step (e.g., parser tolerates but ignores the new line before stamping is enabled).
findings.finding: The plan adds a `rebar-version:` line to produced manifests (a schema change) but provides no explicit rollback/back-out path if the story must be reverted after deployment. Old code encountering new manifests with the extra line is not addressed.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' sub-section to Scope: (1) confirm that compute_validity and verify_signature ignore unrecognised lines by design (cite the parsing logic), so rolled-back code continues to validate new-format manifests; or (2) if they do NOT ignore unknown lines, add an expand-contract step — ship the tolerant parser first, then enable stamping.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the resolver's behavior when `git status --porcelain` fails independently: clarify whether to treat the repo as clean (omit `-dirty`), treat as dirty (append `-dirty`), or drop the SHA entirely — and ensure this is covered by a unit test.
findings.finding: The plan specifies two independent git subprocess calls per resolution (rev-parse then status --porcelain), but does not define the behavior when the second call (`git status --porcelain`) fails or errors independently after the first succeeds.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a clause: 'If rev-parse succeeds but git status fails, treat as non-dirty (omit -dirty suffix) and emit a debug log.' This is the conservative/safe choice and keeps the SHA in the stamp.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify or replace the `git -C <rebar.__file__ dir>` live-git-first approach: either (a) explicitly acknowledge that wheel-install deployments will always omit the SHA (version-only) and confirm Story 2's build-baked path is the real delivery vehicle, or (b) consider surfacing a build-baked `_build_info.py` stub in Story 1 (consistent with `hatchling` already being the build backend) so the resolver has a non-git fallback that works in production.
findings.finding: The plan's primary SHA-resolution mechanism (`git -C <dir of rebar.__file__> rev-parse --short HEAD`) is a novel architectural pattern for this codebase (all existing git calls target the *user's repo*, never rebar's own install directory), and is not justified against the established alternative: `importlib.metadata` already supplies `rebar.__version__` (line 40 of `__init__.py`), and VCS-backed metadata tools (e.g., `hatch-vcs`, already using hatchling per `pyproject.toml:2-3`) can bake a SHA at build time. More critically, in a standard wheel install `rebar.__file__` resolves inside `site-packages` — no `.git` directory exists there — so the git call silently fails on every production deployment, not just edge cases. The plan treats this as a 'silent-failure guard' but does not acknowledge it as the normal wheel-install behavior, nor explain why live-git-first is preferred over the established `importlib.metadata`/build-baked approach that Story 2 will introduce anyway.
findings.location: What section — SHA/version resolver description; 'live-git-first, omit-last (build-baked fallback = story 2)'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
